# Permit portfolios and sectoral counterfactual under Ley~21.752 {#appendix-portfolio}

This appendix reports two pieces of descriptive evidence that underpin
the institutional mechanism developed in Sections~\ref{sec:discussion}.
Section~\ref{appendix-portfolio-permits} documents the revealed
multi-species portfolio held by each sector, refuting the common reading
that the artisanal--industrial asymmetry estimated in the main text is
driven by single-species permit rigidity at the vessel level.
Section~\ref{appendix-counterfactual} computes a policy counterfactual
in which the projected $\mathrm{SSP}5$-$8.5$ end-of-century catch
allocation is recomputed under the Ley~N$^{\circ}$~21.752 fractioning
schedule [@Ley21752-2025], in force from $2026$ through $2040$, and
contrasts it with the Article~$47$~LGPA regime that prevailed during
the estimation window.

## Multi-species permit portfolios in both sectors {#appendix-portfolio-permits}

A natural concern with the institutional reading of our main results is
that artisanal vessels may operate under more restrictive permit
portfolios than industrial vessels, in which case the headline asymmetry
would reflect within-vessel rigidity rather than the cross-sector wedge.
Two complementary data sources allow us to test this directly. For the
industrial sector we use the registry of vessel-level participation
coefficients (\emph{coeficientes de participación relativa}) for jurel,
sardina española and anchoveta, vigent through $2012$, released by
SUBPESCA in response to the Ley de Transparencia request~AH002T-0007379
[@SUBPESCA2012-LTP]. This is the last pre-LTP snapshot of authorised
industrial portfolios and remains the legal basis for the Class~A LTP
coefficients in force thereafter. For the artisanal sector, the
equivalent administrative registry of permits by RPA and species is not
publicly available; we therefore use the $2001$--$2024$ IFOP logbook
($454{,}308$ trip~records on $1{,}583$ vessels covering $81$
species) and treat the set of species each vessel
\emph{actually landed} as a revealed-preference lower bound on the
species for which it holds operational authorisation, since vessels
cannot legally land species outside their authorised portfolio.

Table~\ref{tab:portfolio-descriptive} reports four portfolio-breadth
indicators by sector bucket: the mean number of distinct species
landed over the panel lifetime, the share of vessels landing two or more
species over that lifetime, the mean number of distinct species landed
per vessel-year, and the share of vessel-years with two or more species
active.

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{Permit-portfolio breadth indicators by sector bucket, Chile small pelagic logbook 2001--2024 (artisanal sub-buckets) and SUBPESCA 2012 industrial registry (industrial bucket).\label{tab:portfolio-descriptive}}
\begin{threeparttable}
\begin{tabular}{lrrrrr}
\toprule
Bucket & $n$ vessels & Species lifetime & Multi-species lifetime (\%) & Species per year & Multi-species per year (\%) \\
\midrule
Industrial (IND)            & $129$  & $7.63$ & $87.6$ & $4.14$ & $96.9$ \\
Lancha Menor (LM)           & $888$  & $6.27$ & $89.8$ & $3.81$ & $89.9$ \\
Unclassified artisanal      & $367$  & $4.76$ & $89.6$ & $3.46$ & $91.3$ \\
Bote Menor (BM)             & $189$  & $2.44$ & $63.0$ & $2.14$ & $63.4$ \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\begin{tablenotes}[para,flushleft]
\footnotesize
\item \emph{Notes.} The IND row is constructed from the SUBPESCA $2012$ registry, which lists
$1{,}149$ (vessel~$\times$~species~$\times$~macrozone) authorisations across $299$ industrial
vessels and $26$ \emph{armadores}. Of those $299$ vessels, $75.6\%$ hold authorisations for
the full anchoveta--sardina--jurel triplet and $67.6\%$ hold authorisations in two or more
macrozones, with a median of four (zone~$\times$~species) authorisation pairs per vessel.
Logbook rows are restricted to lances with non-missing species codes (excluding the
\textit{lance sin captura} code 999). Buckets follow the Kasperski-aligned vessel-type
classification used in the main text (Section~\ref{sec:tripresults}): LM is \emph{Lancha
Menor}, BM is \emph{Bote Menor}, and \textit{Unclassified artisanal} pools the remaining
artisanal vessel types. Species-portfolio metrics are computed over the full $81$-species
range in the logbook; the SPF triplet (anchoveta, sardina común, jurel) accounts for
$80$--$86\%$ of artisanal lances and $67\%$ of industrial lances within the panel.
\end{tablenotes}
\end{threeparttable}
\end{table}

Three substantive readings of Table~\ref{tab:portfolio-descriptive}
support the institutional argument developed in
Section~\ref{sec:discussion}. First, the count of permits per vessel
does not distinguish the two sectors in a way that would generate
the headline asymmetry: $87.6\%$ of industrial vessels and $89.8\%$ of
the dominant Lancha Menor sub-bucket of the artisanal sector hold
authorisations for two or more species over the panel lifetime, with
similar shares per vessel-year. Second, the within-artisanal
heterogeneity that anchors the LM--BM contrast in
Table~\ref{tab:tripcompstatemb} is \emph{not} explained by greater
species diversification in BM: BM in fact reports the narrowest
logbook portfolio (mean $2.44$ species lifetime, $2.14$ per year), so
the smaller projected response of BM to climate stress cannot be a
permit-diversification story. The most plausible alternative is that
BM operates substantially outside the regulated logbook
(coastal multi-species and benthic activity), so the projected
trip elasticity applies to a smaller fraction of BM's revealed
livelihood than of LM's. Third, the composition of the multi-species
portfolio differs sharply: $80$--$86\%$ of artisanal lances and $67\%$
of industrial lances fall on the anchoveta--sardina--jurel triplet, but
within that triplet artisanal activity concentrates on the
anchoveta--sardina pair while industrial activity is more evenly
spread, with jurel and caballa together absorbing $\sim 70\%$ of
industrial lances. Under climate forcing, the anchoveta--sardina pair
responds with the identified negative semi-elasticities of
Section~\ref{sec:stock-dynamics}, while jurel productivity is
non-identified at the Centro-Sur scale (Appendix~\ref{appendix-spatial});
the asymmetric concentration of the artisanal portfolio in the
anchoveta--sardina pair therefore translates the same climate forcing
into a sharper effective reduction of the artisanal portfolio than of
the industrial portfolio, conditional on the institutional inability
to recompose access across the sectoral boundary.

## Policy counterfactual under Ley~21.752 {#appendix-counterfactual}

The estimation window of the present paper (2018--2024) coincides with
the Article~$47$ LGPA fractioning regime. Ley~N$^{\circ}$~21.752,
promulgated on $25$~June~$2025$ [@Ley21752-2025], fixes a new schedule
of artisanal--industrial fractions in force through $2040$. Across
the V--Los~Lagos macrozone studied here, the law raises the artisanal
share to $90\%$ in anchoveta and sardina com\'un (from $78\%$) and
partitions jurel into a $30\%$ artisanal share in V--Los~R\'ios and
$15\%$ in Los~Lagos (from a uniform $10\%$ under Article~$47$);
Table~\ref{tab:fractioning} reports the two regimes side by side.
A static plug-in evaluation of the climate-conditional artisanal
landing under each regime is reported in
Section~\ref{sec:ley21752-implications} of the main text
(Table~\ref{tab:counterfactual-main}); this appendix records the
formal derivation and the posterior-propagated, by-model version
that complements that headline table.

Letting $Q_s^{0}$ denote the $2024$ Cuota Global de Captura for
stock~$s$ and $\Delta r_s^{\star}/r_s^{0}$ the projected productivity
change at $\mathrm{SSP}5$-$8.5$ end-of-century, the artisanal
absolute landing under fractioning regime~$R$ is

\begin{equation}
L_{\text{art}}^{(R)} \;=\; \sum_{s} \alpha_s^{(R)} \cdot \big(1 + \Delta r_s^{\star}/r_s^{0}\big) \cdot Q_s^{0},
\label{eq:counterfactual}
\end{equation}

where $\alpha_s^{(R)}$ is the artisanal fraction of stock~$s$ under
regime~$R \in \{\text{Art.}~47, \text{Ley }21.752\}$ and the sum
runs over the four (stock~$\times$~macrozone) units of
Table~\ref{tab:fractioning}: anchoveta V--Los~Lagos, sardina com\'un
V--Los~Lagos, jurel V--Los~R\'ios, and jurel Los~R\'ios--Los~Lagos.
Equation~\eqref{eq:counterfactual} reads the productivity change as a
proportional adjustment to the steady-state landed volume and
abstracts from any endogenous TAC-setting response. The plug-in
version reported in the main text fixes $\Delta r_s^{\star}/r_s^{0}$
at the cross-model posterior median from
Table~\ref{tab:growth_compstat}; the posterior-propagated version
reported below integrates explicitly over the CMIP6 six-model
ensemble and the within-posterior $90\%$ envelope, returning
cross-model median plus inter-quartile range for the central estimate
and the median within-posterior $90\%$ CI for the structural
uncertainty axis, exactly as in
Table~\ref{tab:trip_compstat}.

```{r appendix-counterfactual-table, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE, results='asis'}

# Posterior-propagated counterfactual produced by R/run_counterfactual_ley21752.R
# Format of summary CSV (one row per (stock, regime)):
#   stock_id, regime, cross_med, cross_q25, cross_q75, within_q05, within_q95, n_models
# When the script is run, this chunk renders the appendix table directly from disk.

cf_path <- "tables/appendix_h_counterfactual_ley21752.csv"

if (file.exists(cf_path)) {

  cf <- readr::read_csv(cf_path, show_col_types = FALSE)

  stock_label <- c(
    anchoveta_cs     = "Anchoveta (V--Los~Lagos)",
    sardina_comun_cs = "Sardina com\\'un (V--Los~Lagos)",
    jurel_cs         = "Jurel (V--Los~Lagos, aggregate)",
    TOTAL_CS         = "\\textbf{Total Centro-Sur}"
  )

  cf <- cf %>%
    dplyr::mutate(
      Stock  = stock_label[stock_id],
      Regime = ifelse(regime == "Art47", "Art.~47 LGPA", "Ley~21.752"),
      Central = sprintf("$%.1f$ $[%.1f, %.1f]$", cross_med, cross_q25, cross_q75),
      WithinCI = sprintf("$[%.1f, %.1f]$", within_q05, within_q95)
    ) %>%
    dplyr::arrange(factor(stock_id,
        levels = c("anchoveta_cs","sardina_comun_cs","jurel_cs","TOTAL_CS")),
        regime) %>%
    dplyr::select(Stock, Regime, `Cross-model med [IQR] (kt)` = Central,
                  `Within-posterior 90\\% CI (kt)` = WithinCI)

  # Render manually with threeparttable + footnotes (avoids stargazer wrapper)
  tbl <- knitr::kable(cf, format = "latex", booktabs = TRUE, escape = FALSE,
                      linesep = "")

  cap_str <- paste0(
    "Posterior-propagated artisanal absolute landing under the ",
    "Article~$47$~LGPA and Ley~$21.752$ fractioning regimes, ",
    "$\\mathrm{SSP}5$-$8.5$ end-of-century, Centro-Sur (V--Los~Lagos), ",
    "$2024$ Cuotas Globales.")

  notes_str <- paste0(
    "\\emph{Notes.} Cross-model median and IQR are taken across the ",
    "six CMIP6 models of the per-model posterior median; the within-posterior ",
    "90\\% CI is the median across models of the per-model posterior ",
    "$[q_{05}, q_{95}]$. The construction parallels Table~\\ref{tab:trip_compstat}; ",
    "see \\texttt{R/run\\_counterfactual\\_ley21752.R} for the pipeline. ",
    "Jurel is held at $\\Delta r^{\\star}/r^{0} = 0$ by the ",
    "non-identification convention of Section~\\ref{sec:stock-dynamics}; ",
    "anchoveta and sardina com\\'un use the by-model summaries written by ",
    "the main T5 pipeline (\\texttt{tables/growth\\_comparative\\_statics\\_by\\_model.csv})."
  )

  cat("\\begin{table}[H]\n")
  cat("\\centering\n")
  cat(sprintf("\\caption{%s\\label{tab:counterfactual}}\n", cap_str))
  cat("\\begin{threeparttable}\n")
  cat(as.character(tbl))
  cat("\n\\begin{tablenotes}[flushleft]\\footnotesize\n")
  cat(sprintf("\\item %s\n", notes_str))
  cat("\\end{tablenotes}\n")
  cat("\\end{threeparttable}\n")
  cat("\\end{table}\n")

} else {

  cat("\\noindent\\textit{Posterior-propagated counterfactual table not yet built. ",
      "Run \\texttt{Rscript R/run\\_counterfactual\\_ley21752.R} to generate ",
      "\\texttt{tables/appendix\\_h\\_counterfactual\\_ley21752.csv}, then re-knit.}\n")

}

```

Three readings of the posterior-propagated counterfactual sharpen the
plug-in headline reported in the main text. First, the cross-model
inter-quartile range is narrow at the aggregate level (a few kilotonnes
on either side of the $122$~kt median total), reflecting the floor-effect
saturation documented in Appendix~\ref{appendix-trips-ensemble}: once
the small-pelagic productivity collapses, the cross-model dispersion of
$L_{\text{art}}$ is small in absolute terms because the residual base on
which it operates is small. Second, the within-posterior $90\%$ CI is
substantially wider for anchoveta than for sardina com\'un, because
the posterior on $(\rho^{SST}_{\text{anch}}, \rho^{CHL}_{\text{anch}})$
admits a wider envelope of climate-conditional productivity factors
than the sardina pair. Third, the jurel contribution to the artisanal
landing is invariant across both axes of uncertainty---it appears as a
point in the table---because the non-identification convention holds
$(1 + \Delta r_{\text{jurel}}^{\star}/r_{\text{jurel}}^{0}) = 1$;
relaxing this convention to the ENSO-prior-propagated envelope of
Appendix~\ref{sec:appendix-spatial-enso} would add a third source of
uncertainty whose magnitude is bounded by the three-orders-of-magnitude
range reported there. The plug-in headline in the main text is therefore
robust to the cross-model and within-posterior axes that the appendix
makes explicit, and the institutional reading developed in
Section~\ref{sec:discussion} carries over unchanged.


## Inter-sectoral cession evidence from SERNAPESCA quota control reports {#sec:cesiones-evidence}

This subsection documents the realised inter-sectoral cession flow under
Article~$55$~T of the LGPA, the single administrative re-allocation
channel admitted by the de jure fractioning regime. The data source is
the annual SERNAPESCA quota control report, published as an Excel
workbook for each pair (anchoveta--sardina com\'un V--X) and
(jurel XV--X). For each species--year we extract the assigned, ceded,
effective, and captured tonnage by sector from the workbook's summary
sheet (\emph{RESUMEN} from 2019 onward; per-titular detail rows for
2013--2017, where the workbook format predates the consolidated summary
sheet). The 2018 record is obtained from a SERNAPESCA per-titular CSV transparency
extract, parsed analogously to the workbook series.

Two qualitative facts emerge from the consolidated $2013$--$2024$
series, reported in Table~\ref{tab:cesiones-evidence}. First, in the
anchoveta and sardina com\'un fisheries the industrial sector cedes the
vast majority of its assigned TAC to artisanal cessionaries every year:
the share of the industrial assignment transferred to artisanal
operators ranges from $85\%$ to $99\%$ in $2019$--$2024$ and is
documented at lower bounds of $11$--$72\%$ in the format-heterogeneous
$2013$--$2017$ workbooks. The cession volume scales with the TAC and
ranges from $26$ to $50$~kt per year in anchoveta and from $51$ to
$77$~kt per year in sardina com\'un. Second, in the jurel fishery the
cession flow is an order of magnitude smaller relative to the assigned
TAC ($-3.5$ to $+4.3\%$) and reverses direction across years: $2019$--$2022$
records small artisanal-to-industrial cessions of $11$--$15$~kt per year,
which reverse to industrial-to-artisanal cessions of $0.7$ and $31.5$~kt
in $2023$ and $2024$ respectively.

The implication for the main-text policy interpretation is twofold.
First, the structural asymmetry that the paper identifies is estimated
on realised landings, which are post-cession; the asymmetry therefore
reflects the operational allocation produced by the joint
Article~$47$--Article~$55$~T regime rather than the de jure
Article~$47$ fractioning alone. Second, the static counterfactual under
Ley~$21.752$ in Section~\ref{sec:ley21752-implications} of the main
text and in equation~\eqref{eq:counterfactual} of this appendix should
be read as a de jure upper bound on the operational re-allocation:
because the industrial sector already cedes the bulk of its assigned
small-pelagic TAC to artisanal operators under the pre-reform regime,
the $12$~percentage-point reduction in the industrial de jure share
under items~$6$ and~$7$ of Article~$1$ of Ley~$21.752$ translates into a
proportionally smaller change in realised artisanal landings. The
operationally meaningful change in the small pelagics is the
re-pricing of the Article~$55$~T cession channel under the $1.3$~UTM/ton
patent of Article~$6$ rather than the de jure share reassignment.

\begin{table}[H]
\centering
\caption{Inter-sectoral cession flow under Article~$55$~T of the LGPA,
Central-South small pelagic fishery, $2013$--$2024$. Source:
SERNAPESCA quota control reports.\label{tab:cesiones-evidence}}
\begin{threeparttable}
\begin{tabular}{lrrrrr}
\toprule
Year & Species & IND assigned (kt) & IND cession (kt) & Share ceded & Direction \\
\midrule
2018 & anchoveta &  12.8 & $-10.7$ & 84\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2018 & sardina   &  74.2 & $-63.2$ & 85\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2018 & jurel     & 326.9 & $+16.1$ &  5\% & ART$\rightarrow$IND \\
2019 & anchoveta &  27.4 & $-25.8$ & 94\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2019 & sardina   &  76.0 & $-69.6$ & 92\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2019 & jurel     & 339.0 & $+12.0$ &  3\% & ART$\rightarrow$IND \\
2020 & anchoveta &  38.6 & $-33.3$ & 86\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2020 & sardina   &  67.2 & $-60.9$ & 91\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2020 & jurel     &1268.1 & $+11.8$ &  1\% & ART$\rightarrow$IND \\
2021 & anchoveta &  45.3 & $-45.1$ &$\sim\!100\%$ & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2021 & sardina   &  76.9 & $-76.5$ &$\sim\!100\%$ & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2021 & jurel     & 448.7 & $+13.3$ &  3\% & ART$\rightarrow$IND \\
2022 & anchoveta &  52.2 & $-50.5$ & 97\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2022 & sardina   &  77.7 & $-71.6$ & 92\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2022 & jurel     & 516.4 & $+15.2$ &  3\% & ART$\rightarrow$IND \\
2023 & anchoveta &  39.9 & $-37.1$ & 93\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2023 & sardina   &  63.3 & $-61.7$ & 98\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2023 & jurel     & 637.0 & $-0.7$  &  0\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2024 & anchoveta &  53.8 & $-45.5$ & 85\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2024 & sardina   &  53.9 & $-51.0$ & 95\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
2024 & jurel     & 728.6 & $-31.5$ &  4\% & IND$\rightarrow$ART \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\begin{tablenotes}[flushleft]\footnotesize
\item \emph{Notes.} ``IND assigned'' is the industrial fraction of the
Cuota Global de Captura aggregated across sub-macrozones (anchoveta and
sardina com\'un: V--Los~Lagos; jurel: XV--Los~Lagos). ``IND cession''
sums the \emph{Movimientos} column across industrial regional rows of
the SERNAPESCA \emph{RESUMEN} sheet; negative values denote
industrial-to-artisanal flows under Article~$55$~T, positive values
denote the reverse direction. ``Share ceded'' is the absolute ratio
$|\text{cession}|/\text{IND assigned}$. The $2018$ report was obtained as a per-titular CSV transparency extract from
SERNAPESCA and is parsed analogously to the $2019$--$2024$ workbooks. Source workbooks for $2013$--$2017$ use a
different layout (per-titular per-period detail without a consolidated
summary sheet); cession totals for that subperiod, available on
request, are qualitatively consistent with the pattern reported here.
The full per-year, per-sub-macrozone CSV is archived in
\texttt{paper1/portfolio\_check/cesiones\_intersectoral\_2019\_2024.csv}
and \texttt{cesiones\_consolidated\_2013\_2024.csv}.
\end{tablenotes}
\end{threeparttable}
\end{table}
